topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink swam before my eyes, a mocking indictment splashed across the top of the graded essay. A C-minus. I stared at the paper, my hand shaking so violently I had to grip the desk edge to steady myself. Every word I had poured onto that page, every late night fueled by lukewarm coffee and sheer panic, had resulted in this dismal verdict. My stomach twisted into a cold, tight knot. I slowly pushed the paper away, the sound of the paper sliding across the desk echoing unnaturally loud in the silent room. I could feel a strange, hollow pressure building behind my ribs, making it hard to draw a full breath. I stood up, my legs feeling strangely disconnected from my body, and walked toward the door, needing air, needing distance from the stark reality of those numbers.

[story 2]

"A D?" The words barely made it past my throat. I reread the feedback, the instructor’s terse critique seeming to vibrate with judgment. My palms were slick, clammy against the cool laminate of my laptop. I kept glancing at the clock on the wall; each second stretched into an agonizing eternity. I pressed my forehead against the cool surface of the desk, trying to absorb some of the stillness, but the silence only amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. My vision tunneled slightly at the edges. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to mentally rewind the last three weeks, searching for the single mistake that had derailed everything. When I finally forced my eyes open, the room seemed too bright, the shadows too deep, and a wave of nausea washed over me, leaving me breathless and shaky.

[story 3]

I couldn't look at the transcript. It was a landscape of failure, the lowest mark I had ever seen. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid, catching somewhere in my chest. I started pacing the small confines of my room, my movements jerky and uncontrolled. Each footfall on the wooden floor sounded like a hammer blow. My heart hammered against my ribs with such force that I worried the sound might escape. I stopped abruptly by the window, staring out at the indifferent streetlights below. The air felt thick, suffocating. I pressed my back against the glass, trying to physically anchor myself against the sudden, overwhelming urge to simply disappear. The world outside seemed impossibly distant and uncaring.
